agentic-flow-complete_2.title: Overview
agentic-flow-complete_2.content:
**Agentic Flow** is an advanced AI agent framework that combines performance optimization with learning capabilities. It is described as "The First AI Agent Framework That Gets Smarter AND Faster Every Time It Runs."

**Key Statistics:**
- 150+ specialized agents
- 213 MCP tools
- 352x faster code operations (via Agent Booster)
- 46% faster execution with learning (via ReasoningBank)
- Built on Claude Agent SDK by Anthropic

**Package:** `agentic-flow` on npm
**License:** MIT
**Node.js Requirement:** >=18.0.0
**Repository:** https://github.com/ruvnet/agentic-flow

---

agentic-flow-complete_22.title: 1. Agent Booster
agentic-flow-complete_22.content:
Ultra-fast local code transformations via Rust/WASM (auto-detects edits).

**Performance:**
- Single edit: 352ms to 1ms (352x improvement)
- 100 edits: 35 seconds to 0.1 seconds
- 1000 files: 5.87 minutes to 1 second
- Cost: $0.01/edit to $0.00 (100% free)

**Usage:** Automatic on code edits, or import via `agentic-flow/agent-booster`

agentic-flow-complete_33.title: 2. AgentDB v2
agentic-flow-complete_33.content:
RuVector-powered graph database with vector search, GNN learning, and comprehensive diagnostics.

**Performance:**
- 150x faster than SQLite
- Sub-millisecond latency
- HNSW indexing for 116x faster vector search
- Batch operations 141x faster

**CLI Commands:**
```bash
npx agentdb@alpha init --dimension 768 --preset medium
npx agentdb@alpha doctor --verbose
npx agentdb@alpha reflexion store "session-1" "implement_auth" 0.95 true
npx agentdb@alpha reflexion retrieve "authentication" --synthesize-context
npx agentdb@alpha skill search "authentication" 10
npx agentdb@alpha migrate legacy.db --target new-v2.db
```

**Features:**
- 29 MCP tools for Claude Desktop integration
- Reflexion memory (5 tools)
- Skill library (5 tools)
- Causal memory graph (5 tools)
- RL learning system (10 tools, 9 algorithms)

agentic-flow-complete_59.title: 3. ReasoningBank
agentic-flow-complete_59.content:
Persistent learning memory system with semantic search.

**Performance:**
- 46% faster execution
- 100% success rate improvement over iterations
- 32.3% token reduction

**Learning Cycle:**
```
RETRIEVE -> JUDGE -> DISTILL -> CONSOLIDATE
```

**Usage:**
```javascript
import * as reasoningbank from 'agentic-flow/reasoningbank';
await reasoningbank.initialize();
await reasoningbank.storeMemory('pattern_name', 'pattern_value', { namespace: 'api' });
const results = await reasoningbank.queryMemories('search query', { namespace: 'api' });
```

agentic-flow-complete_80.title: 4. Multi-Model Router
agentic-flow-complete_80.content:
Intelligent cost optimization across 100+ LLMs.

**Savings:** 85-99% cost reduction

**Model Tiers:**
- **Tier 1 (Flagship):** Claude Sonnet 4.5 ($3/$15 per 1M), GPT-4o ($2.50/$10 per 1M)
- **Tier 2 (Cost-Effective):** DeepSeek R1 ($0.55/$2.19 per 1M - 85% cheaper)
- **Tier 3 (Balanced):** Gemini 2.5 Flash ($0.07/$0.30 per 1M)
- **Tier 4 (Budget):** Llama 3.1 8B ($0.055/$0.055 per 1M)
- **Tier 5 (Local/Privacy):** ONNX Phi-4 (FREE, offline)

**Usage:**
```bash
npx agentic-flow --agent coder --task "Build REST API" --optimize
npx agentic-flow --agent coder --task "Simple function" --optimize --priority cost
npx agentic-flow --agent reviewer --task "Security audit" --optimize --priority quality
```

agentic-flow-complete_99.title: 5. QUIC Transport
agentic-flow-complete_99.content:
Ultra-low latency agent communication via Rust/WASM QUIC protocol.

**Performance:**
- 50-70% faster than TCP
- 0-RTT (instant) connection
- 100+ concurrent streams
- Built-in TLS 1.3 encryption

**Usage:**
```bash
npx agentic-flow quic --port 4433
```

agentic-flow-complete_113.title: 6. Federation Hub
agentic-flow-complete_113.content:
Ephemeral agents (5s-15min lifetime) with persistent cross-agent memory.

**Usage:**
```bash
npx agentic-flow federation start
npx agentic-flow federation spawn
npx agentic-flow federation stats
```

agentic-flow-complete_123.title: 7. Swarm Optimization
agentic-flow-complete_123.content:
Self-learning parallel execution with AI topology selection.

**Performance:** 3-5x speedup with auto-optimization

agentic-flow-complete_472.title: Overview
agentic-flow-complete_472.content: ReasoningBank is a closed-loop learning and reasoning framework that enables agents to learn from experience. Based on research achieving 0% to 100% success transformation.

agentic-flow-complete_477.title: Phase 1: RETRIEVE
agentic-flow-complete_477.content:
Before execution, retrieve relevant memories:
- 4-factor scoring: similarity (65%), recency (15%), reliability (20%), diversity (10%)
- Query expansion with key concepts
- Returns top k=3 most relevant memories

agentic-flow-complete_483.title: Phase 2: EXECUTE
agentic-flow-complete_483.content:
Apply retrieved insights:
- Use learned patterns
- Avoid known failure patterns
- Apply confidence-based decisions

agentic-flow-complete_489.title: Phase 3: JUDGE
agentic-flow-complete_489.content:
Post-execution analysis:
- Evaluate outcome success/failure
- Analyze what went wrong
- Assess confidence levels

agentic-flow-complete_495.title: Phase 4: DISTILL
agentic-flow-complete_495.content:
Extract reusable learnings:
- Identify patterns
- Generalize insights
- Enrich metadata
- Store for future use

agentic-flow-complete_502.title: Consolidation
agentic-flow-complete_502.content:
Every ~100 patterns:
- Merge similar patterns
- Prune obsolete strategies
- Abstract concepts to higher-level heuristics

agentic-flow-complete_761.title: With OpenRouter (99% cost savings)

agentic-security-complete_2.title: Overview
agentic-security-complete_2.content:
**Agentic Security** is a fully autonomous AI-powered security pipeline that combines advanced AI tools with OWASP ZAP integration to streamline security scanning, remediation, and code management for modern development environments. It provides comprehensive security across code, architecture, and DevOps with hands-free vulnerability detection, intelligent fixes, and seamless DevSecOps integration.

**Repository**: https://github.com/ruvnet/agentic-security
**Created by**: rUv

---

agentic-security-complete_13.title: 1. Auto-Fix/Coding Pipeline
agentic-security-complete_13.content:
Hands-free, AI-driven remediation handling vulnerabilities from discovery to fix.

| Capability | Benefits |
|------------|----------|
| Automated Code Remediation | Faster, automated fixes |
| Self-Learning System | Improved accuracy over time |
| Intelligent Fix Validation | Minimizes regression risks |
| Zero-Day Vulnerability Protection | Readiness for emerging threats |

agentic-security-complete_23.title: 2. Enterprise-Grade Security Integration
agentic-security-complete_23.content:
Seamless DevSecOps integration for constant security monitoring.

| Capability | Benefits |
|------------|----------|
| DevSecOps Integration | Minimal workflow disruption |
| Compliance Checks | Automated compliance maintenance |
| Security Gates | Continuous enforcement |
| Real-Time Monitoring | Immediate threat response |

agentic-security-complete_33.title: 3. Comprehensive Security Checks
agentic-security-complete_33.content:
Protection via OWASP ZAP, Nuclei, and dependency checks, aligned with OWASP Top 10.

| Capability | Benefits |
|------------|----------|
| Web Vulnerability Scans | Broad coverage |
| Exploit Detection | Known vulnerability protection |
| Dependency Checks | Mitigates outdated components |
| OWASP Compliance | Best security practices |

agentic-security-complete_47.title: Core Components
agentic-security-complete_47.content:
1. **SecurityPipeline** - The main orchestrator managing security scanning and fixing
2. **CLI Interface** - Cyberpunk-styled command-line interface
3. **Cache System** - Performance optimization through result caching
4. **AI Integration** - GPT-4 and Claude for code analysis and fixes
5. **Fix Cycle** - Iterative vulnerability remediation system
6. **Progress Reporter** - Real-time progress tracking

agentic-security-complete_56.title: Data Flow
agentic-security-complete_56.content:
```
1. Input Processing
   - Configuration loading from YAML
   - Environment validation
   - Path scanning configuration

2. Security Scanning
   - Code security checks
   - Web security scanning (ZAP, Nuclei)
   - Dependency vulnerability checking
   - Architecture review

3. Analysis & Remediation
   - Vulnerability severity assessment
   - AI-powered fix generation
   - Fix validation
   - Pull request creation

4. Reporting
   - Markdown report generation
   - Console output
   - Slack notifications
```

agentic-security-complete_82.content:

security_patterns:

    - "SELECT * FROM"
    - "INSERT INTO"

    - "exec("

    - "DES"

agentic-security-complete_123.title: Vulnerability Types Detected
agentic-security-complete_123.content:
1. **SQL Injection** - String formatting in SQL queries
2. **Command Injection** - RCE vulnerabilities
3. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** - DOM manipulation
4. **Weak Cryptography** - Insecure hash algorithms
5. **Insecure Deserialization** - pickle, yaml vulnerabilities
6. **XXE** - XML Parser vulnerabilities
7. **Path Traversal** - File access issues
8. **Authentication Weaknesses** - Session management issues

agentic-security-complete_357.title: GitHub Actions Integration
agentic-security-complete_357.content:
```yaml
name: Security Pipeline

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 5 * * *'  # Daily at 5 AM

jobs:
  security-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      security-events: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install --user -r requirements.txt

      - name: Install Security Tools
        run: |
          docker pull owasp/zap2docker-stable
          # Install Nuclei, Dependency-Check

      - name: Run security pipeline
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          ./run_pipeline.sh

      - name: Create Pull Request
        uses: peter-evans/create-pull-request@v5
        with:
          title: "security: Automated fixes for vulnerabilities"
          labels: security, automated-pr
```

agentic-security-complete_517.title: SQL_INJECTION (HIGH)
agentic-security-complete_517.content:
- Potential SQL injection vulnerability detected
- User input used in SQL queries without proper sanitization

**Recommendations:**
- Use parameterized queries
- Implement input validation
- Use ORM if appropriate

agentic-security-complete_528.title: WEAK_CRYPTO (MEDIUM)
agentic-security-complete_528.content:
- Weak hashing algorithm detected

**Recommendations:**
- Use strong hashing (SHA-256, SHA-512)
- Implement proper salting
- Use established crypto libraries

agentic-security-complete_536.title: Overall Recommendations
agentic-security-complete_536.content:
1. **High Priority:** Address command injection and deserialization issues
2. **Security Best Practices:** Use security-focused libraries
3. **Maintenance:** Keep dependencies updated

api-reference_1323.title: Swarm Topologies
api-reference_1323.content:
```typescript
type SwarmTopology = 'hierarchical' | 'mesh' | 'ring' | 'star';

interface SwarmConfig {
  /**
   * Swarm topology type
   * - hierarchical: Tree structure with coordinator at root
   * - mesh: Fully connected peer-to-peer network
   * - ring: Circular communication pattern
   * - star: Central hub with spoke agents
   */
  topology: SwarmTopology;

  /**
   * Maximum number of agents in swarm
   * @default 8
   */
  maxAgents?: number;

  /**
   * Agent distribution strategy
   * - auto: Automatically select based on task
   * - balanced: Even distribution across capabilities
   * - specialized: Group by specialization
   */
  strategy?: 'auto' | 'balanced' | 'specialized' | 'adaptive';
}
```

**Topology Characteristics:**

| Topology | Best For | Communication | Fault Tolerance |
|----------|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Hierarchical | Large teams, clear delegation | O(log n) | Medium |
| Mesh | Complex collaboration | O(n) | High |
| Ring | Sequential workflows | O(n) | Low |
| Star | Centralized coordination | O(1) to hub | Medium |

api-reference_2104.title: 4.1 HybridMemoryArchitecture
api-reference_2104.content:
The hybrid memory system combines vector, episodic, semantic, and working memory for comprehensive knowledge management.

```python
from safla import HybridMemoryArchitecture
from safla.core.hybrid_memory import (
    VectorMemoryManager,
    SimilarityMetric,
    EvictionPolicy,
    MemoryItem,
    EpisodicEvent,
    SemanticNode
)

class HybridMemoryArchitecture:
    """
    Hybrid memory architecture combining multiple memory types
    for comprehensive knowledge management.
    """

    def __init__(self, config: Optional[MemoryConfig] = None):
        """
        Initialize the hybrid memory architecture.

        Args:
            config: Optional configuration for memory subsystems
        """
        pass

    async def initialize(self) -> None:
        """Initialize all memory subsystems."""
        pass

    # Vector Memory Operations

    async def store_vector(
        self,
        vector: np.ndarray,
        metadata: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
        namespace: str = "default"
    ) -> str:
        """
        Store a vector in vector memory.

        Args:
            vector: The vector to store (numpy array)
            metadata: Optional metadata for the vector
            namespace: Memory namespace

architecture-patterns_51.title: Pattern 1: Optimized MCP Orchestrator
architecture-patterns_51.content:
**Source:** SAFLA/safla/core/mcp_orchestration.py

**Description:** High-performance MCP orchestration with connection pooling, circuit breaker, and caching.

**Key Components:**
- MCPCache with LRU eviction and TTL
- CircuitBreaker for fault tolerance
- MCPConnectionPool with auto-scaling
- Priority queue for request handling

```python
class OptimizedMCPOrchestrator:
    """
    High-performance MCP orchestrator with advanced optimization techniques.

    Performance Targets:
    - MCP round-trip latency: <5ms for standard operations
    - Concurrent request handling: 100+ requests/second
    - Intelligent caching and connection pooling
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        base_url: str = "http://localhost:8000",
        max_concurrent_requests: int = 100,
        request_timeout: float = 5.0,
        cache_size: int = 1000,
        cache_ttl: float = 300.0
    ):
        self.cache = MCPCache(max_size=cache_size, default_ttl=cache_ttl)
        self.connection_pool = MCPConnectionPool()
        self.circuit_breaker = CircuitBreaker()
        self.request_semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent_requests)
```

**Use Cases:**
- High-throughput AI inference systems
- Real-time agent coordination
- Low-latency code analysis pipelines

architecture-patterns_95.title: Pattern 2: MCP Connection Pool
architecture-patterns_95.content:
**Source:** SAFLA/safla/core/mcp_orchestration.py

**Description:** Connection pooling for efficient resource management.

```python
class MCPConnectionPool:
    def __init__(
        self,
        min_connections: int = 5,
        max_connections: int = 50,
        connection_timeout: float = 10.0
    ):
        self.min_connections = min_connections
        self.max_connections = max_connections
        self.available_connections: queue.Queue = queue.Queue()
        self.active_connections: weakref.WeakSet = weakref.WeakSet()

    async def get_connection(self) -> aiohttp.ClientSession:
        try:
            connection = self.available_connections.get_nowait()
            self.active_connections.add(connection)
            return connection
        except queue.Empty:
            # Create new connection if under limit
            if self.total_connections < self.max_connections:
                connection = self._create_connection()
                self.total_connections += 1
                return connection
```

**Key Features:**
- Automatic scaling between min/max connections
- WeakRef for active connection tracking
- Non-blocking connection acquisition
- Graceful connection return and cleanup

architecture-patterns_165.title: Pattern 4: MCP Response Caching
architecture-patterns_165.content:
**Source:** SAFLA/safla/core/mcp_orchestration.py

```python
class MCPCache:
    def __init__(self, max_size: int = 1000, default_ttl: float = 300.0):
        self.max_size = max_size
        self.default_ttl = default_ttl
        self.cache: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]] = {}
        self.access_order: deque = deque()
        self.lock = threading.RLock()

    def get(self, key: str) -> Optional[Any]:
        with self.lock:
            if key not in self.cache:
                return None
            entry = self.cache[key]
            # Check TTL
            if time.time() > entry['expires_at']:
                del self.cache[key]
                return None
            # Update LRU order
            self.access_order.remove(key)
            self.access_order.append(key)
            return entry['data']
```

**Cache Strategy:**
- TTL-based expiration
- LRU eviction when at capacity
- Thread-safe with RLock
- Pattern-based invalidation support

architecture-patterns_639.title: Pattern 14: Adaptive Safety Boundaries
architecture-patterns_639.content:
**Source:** SAFLA/safla/core/adaptive_safety_boundaries.py

```python
class SafetyLevel(Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"
    HIGH = "high"
    MEDIUM = "medium"
    LOW = "low"
    MINIMAL = "minimal"

class ConstraintType(Enum):
    HARD = "hard"       # Cannot be violated
    SOFT = "soft"       # Can be relaxed under certain conditions
    ADAPTIVE = "adaptive"  # Dynamically adjusted

@dataclass
class SafetyConstraint:
    name: str
    constraint_type: ConstraintType
    threshold: float
    current_value: float = 0.0
    adaptivity_rate: float = 0.1
    min_threshold: float = 0.0
    max_threshold: float = 1.0
    violation_count: int = 0
    last_updated: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.now)

class AdaptiveSafetyBoundaries:
    def __init__(
        self,
        initial_constraints: Optional[List[SafetyConstraint]] = None,
        learning_rate: float = 0.01,
        risk_tolerance: float = 0.1
    ):
        self.constraints: Dict[str, SafetyConstraint] = {}
        self.learning_rate = learning_rate
        self.risk_tolerance = risk_tolerance
        self.violation_history: List[Dict[str, Any]] = []
        self.adaptation_history: List[Dict[str, Any]] = []

    async def _adapt_constraint(self, constraint_name: str) -> None:
        constraint = self.constraints[constraint_name]

        if constraint.violation_count > 3:
            # Relax the constraint slightly
            new_threshold = min(
                constraint.threshold * (1 + constraint.adaptivity_rate),
                constraint.max_threshold
            )
            constraint.threshold = new_threshold
            constraint.violation_count = 0

            self.adaptation_history.append({
                'constraint': constraint_name,
                'old_threshold': constraint.threshold,
                'new_threshold': new_threshold,
                'timestamp': datetime.now()
            })
```

architecture-patterns_763.title: Pattern 16: Circuit Breaker
architecture-patterns_763.content:
**Source:** SAFLA/safla/core/mcp_orchestration.py

```python
class CircuitBreakerState(Enum):
    CLOSED = "closed"
    OPEN = "open"
    HALF_OPEN = "half_open"

class CircuitBreaker:
    def __init__(
        self,
        failure_threshold: int = 5,
        recovery_timeout: float = 60.0,
        expected_exception: type = Exception
    ):
        self.failure_threshold = failure_threshold
        self.recovery_timeout = recovery_timeout
        self.failure_count = 0
        self.last_failure_time = None
        self.state = CircuitBreakerState.CLOSED

    async def acall(self, func: Callable, *args, **kwargs):
        if self.state == CircuitBreakerState.OPEN:
            if self._should_attempt_reset():
                self.state = CircuitBreakerState.HALF_OPEN
            else:
                raise Exception("Circuit breaker is OPEN")

        try:
            result = await func(*args, **kwargs)
            self._on_success()
            return result
        except self.expected_exception as e:
            self._on_failure()
            raise e

    def _on_success(self):
        self.failure_count = 0
        self.state = CircuitBreakerState.CLOSED

    def _on_failure(self):
        self.failure_count += 1
        self.last_failure_time = time.time()
        if self.failure_count >= self.failure_threshold:
            self.state = CircuitBreakerState.OPEN
```

architecture-patterns_1585.title: Pattern 32: Auto-Scaling Connection Pool
architecture-patterns_1585.content:
**Source:** SAFLA/safla/core/mcp_orchestration.py

```python
class MCPConnectionPool:
    def __init__(
        self,
        min_connections: int = 5,
        max_connections: int = 50,
        connection_timeout: float = 10.0
    ):
        self.min_connections = min_connections
        self.max_connections = max_connections
        self.total_connections = 0

    async def get_connection(self) -> aiohttp.ClientSession:
        try:
            # Try to get available connection
            connection = self.available_connections.get_nowait()
            return connection
        except queue.Empty:
            # Scale up if under limit
            with self.lock:
                if self.total_connections < self.max_connections:
                    connection = self._create_connection()
                    self.total_connections += 1
                    return connection
                else:
                    # Wait for available connection
                    connection = self.available_connections.get(timeout=5.0)
                    return connection

    def return_connection(self, connection: aiohttp.ClientSession):
        if not connection.closed:
            try:
                self.available_connections.put_nowait(connection)
            except queue.Full:
                # Pool is full, close excess connection (scale down)
                asyncio.create_task(connection.close())
                with self.lock:
                    self.total_connections -= 1
```

architecture-patterns_1682.title: Pattern 34: Horizontal Scaling with Docker Compose
architecture-patterns_1682.content:
**Source:** agentic-flow/docker/docker-compose.yml

```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  # Base Agentic Flow service
  agentic-flow:
    build:
      context: ..
      dockerfile: docker/base/Dockerfile
    container_name: agentic-flow
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
      - PORT=8080
      - ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}
    volumes:
      - agent-memory:/app/.claude-flow/memory
      - agent-metrics:/app/.claude-flow/metrics
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "node", "dist/health.js"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 3
    restart: unless-stopped

  # Multiple model instances for horizontal scaling
  agentic-flow-claude:
    extends: agentic-flow
    container_name: agentic-flow-claude
    ports:
      - "8081:8080"
    environment:
      - PROVIDER=anthropic
      - COMPLETION_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4

  agentic-flow-llama:
    extends: agentic-flow
    container_name: agentic-flow-llama
    ports:
      - "8082:8080"
    environment:
      - PROVIDER=openrouter
      - USE_OPENROUTER=true
      - COMPLETION_MODEL=meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct

  agentic-flow-deepseek:
    extends: agentic-flow
    container_name: agentic-flow-deepseek
    ports:
      - "8083:8080"
    environment:
      - PROVIDER=openrouter
      - COMPLETION_MODEL=deepseek/deepseek-chat

volumes:
  agent-memory:
    driver: local
  agent-metrics:
    driver: local

networks:
  default:
    name: agentic-flow-network
```

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder

COPY agentic-flow/package*.json ./

FROM node:20-alpine

RUN apk add --no-cache curl ca-certificates tini

architecture-patterns_2156.title: Create non-root user
architecture-patterns_2156.content:
RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs && adduser -S nodejs -u 1001

WORKDIR /app

architecture-patterns_2161.title: Copy from builder
architecture-patterns_2161.content:
COPY --from=builder --chown=nodejs:nodejs /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder --chown=nodejs:nodejs /app/dist ./dist
COPY --chown=nodejs:nodejs agentic-flow/package*.json ./

ENV NODE_ENV=production PORT=8080

USER nodejs

ENTRYPOINT ["/sbin/tini", "--"]
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]

RUN echo '#!/bin/bash' > /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo 'if [ "$1" = "test" ]; then' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo '  exec /app/docker/test-instance/test-runner.sh' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo 'elif [ "$1" = "cli" ]; then' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo '  shift' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo '  exec node /app/dist/cli.js "$@"' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo 'else' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo '  exec "$@"' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    echo 'fi' >> /entrypoint.sh && \
    chmod +x /entrypoint.sh

ENTRYPOINT ["/entrypoint.sh"]

- `query_control` - Control running queries (pause, resume, terminate, change model)
- `query_list` - List all active queries and their status

claude-flow-complete_338.title: Pre-Operation Hooks
claude-flow-complete_338.content:
**pre-edit** - Validate and assign agents before file modifications
```bash
npx claude-flow hook pre-edit --file "src/auth.js" --auto-assign-agent --validate-syntax
```

**pre-bash** - Check command safety and resource requirements
```bash
npx claude-flow hook pre-bash --command "docker build ." --check-safety
```

**pre-task** - Auto-spawn agents and prepare for complex tasks
```bash
npx claude-flow hook pre-task --description "Build REST API" --auto-spawn-agents --load-memory
```

**pre-search** - Prepare and optimize search operations
```bash
npx claude-flow hook pre-search --query "authentication" --check-cache
```

claude-flow-complete_360.title: Post-Operation Hooks
claude-flow-complete_360.content:
**post-edit** - Auto-format, validate, and update memory
```bash
npx claude-flow hook post-edit --file "src/auth.js" --memory-key "auth/login" --auto-format --train-patterns
```

**post-bash** - Log execution and update metrics
```bash
npx claude-flow hook post-bash --command "npm test" --update-metrics
```

**post-task** - Performance analysis and decision storage
```bash
npx claude-flow hook post-task --task-id "auth-impl" --analyze-performance --store-decisions
```

**post-search** - Cache results and improve patterns
```bash
npx claude-flow hook post-search --query "auth" --cache-results --train-patterns
```

claude-flow-complete_382.title: Session Hooks
claude-flow-complete_382.content:
**session-start** - Initialize new session
```bash
npx claude-flow hook session-start --session-id "dev-session" --load-context
```

**session-restore** - Load previous session state
```bash
npx claude-flow hook session-restore --session-id "swarm-20241019" --restore-memory
```

**session-end** - Cleanup and persist session state
```bash
npx claude-flow hook session-end --session-id "dev-session" --export-metrics --generate-summary
```

**notify** - Custom notifications with swarm status
```bash
npx claude-flow hook notify --message "Task completed" --level info --broadcast
```

claude-flow-complete_404.title: MCP Integration Hooks
claude-flow-complete_404.content:
- **mcp-initialized** - Persist swarm configuration
- **agent-spawned** - Update agent roster and memory
- **task-orchestrated** - Monitor task progress
- **neural-trained** - Save pattern improvements

claude-flow-complete_411.title: Memory Coordination Hooks
claude-flow-complete_411.content:
- **memory-write** - Triggered when agents write to coordination memory
- **memory-read** - Triggered when agents read from coordination memory
- **memory-sync** - Synchronize memory across swarm agents

**Features:**
- Semantic vector search (HNSW indexing, O(log n))
- 9 RL algorithms (Q-Learning, PPO, MCTS, Decision Transformer)
- Reflexion memory (learn from past experiences)
- Skill library (auto-consolidate successful patterns)
- Causal reasoning (understand cause-effect relationships)
- Quantization (binary 32x, scalar 4x, product 8-16x reduction)
- 100% backward compatible (hybrid mode with graceful fallback)

  --namespace backend --reasoningbank

claude-flow-complete_505.title: Check status and statistics
claude-flow-complete_505.content:
npx claude-flow@alpha memory status --reasoningbank
```

**Features:**
- No API Keys Required: Hash-based embeddings (1024 dimensions)
- Persistent Storage: SQLite database survives restarts
- Pattern Matching: LIKE-based search with similarity scoring
- Namespace Isolation: Organize memories by domain
- Fast Queries: 2-3ms average latency
- Process Cleanup: Automatic database closing

claude-flow-complete_517.title: Memory Types (Hive Mind)
claude-flow-complete_517.content:
| Type | Description | TTL |
|------|-------------|-----|
| `knowledge` | Permanent insights | No TTL |
| `context` | Session context | 1 hour |
| `task` | Task-specific data | 30 min |
| `result` | Execution results | Permanent, compressed |
| `error` | Error logs | 24 hours |
| `metric` | Performance metrics | 1 hour |
| `consensus` | Decision records | Permanent |
| `system` | System configuration | Permanent |

claude-flow-complete_530.title: Memory Operations

claude-flow-complete_563.title: 1. Centralized Topology
claude-flow-complete_563.content:
- **Structure**: Single coordinator manages all agents
- **Best For**: Simple tasks, clear hierarchies, strong coordination needs
- **Advantages**: Simple coordination, clear authority, easy debugging
- **Disadvantages**: Single point of failure, bottleneck at coordinator

claude-flow-complete_569.title: 2. Distributed Topology
claude-flow-complete_569.content:
- **Structure**: Multiple coordinators share management responsibilities
- **Best For**: Large-scale operations, fault tolerance, geographical distribution
- **Advantages**: High fault tolerance, excellent scalability, load distribution
- **Disadvantages**: Complex coordination, consistency challenges

claude-flow-complete_575.title: 3. Mesh Topology
claude-flow-complete_575.content:
- **Structure**: Peer-to-peer agent network with direct communication
- **Best For**: Collaborative tasks, consensus-driven decisions, research projects
- **Advantages**: Democratic decision-making, high redundancy, self-organizing
- **Disadvantages**: High communication overhead, consensus can be slow

claude-flow-complete_581.title: 4. Hierarchical Topology
claude-flow-complete_581.content:
- **Structure**: Tree-like structure with multiple coordination levels
- **Best For**: Complex projects, clear task breakdown, enterprise scenarios
- **Advantages**: Clear responsibility chains, efficient for complex tasks
- **Disadvantages**: Rigid structure, potential bottlenecks at levels

claude-flow-complete_587.title: 5. Hybrid Topology
claude-flow-complete_587.content:
- **Structure**: Combines multiple topologies for optimal performance
- **Best For**: Complex, multi-phase projects with varying requirements
- **Advantages**: Optimal for each phase, maximum flexibility
- **Disadvantages**: Most complex to implement, higher resource requirements

claude-flow-complete_595.title: Voting Systems
claude-flow-complete_595.content:
- **Simple Majority Voting**: 50% + 1 threshold
- **Weighted Voting**: Agent expertise weights
- **Supermajority Voting**: 2/3 majority for critical decisions

claude-flow-complete_600.title: Consensus Algorithms
claude-flow-complete_600.content:
- **Raft Consensus**: Leader election with log replication
- **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)**: f = (n-1)/3 Byzantine nodes
- **Proof of Stake (PoS)**: Performance/reliability-based staking

claude-flow-complete_605.title: Byzantine Fault Tolerance
claude-flow-complete_605.content:
The swarm system handles Byzantine failures through:
- Agent Authentication and Trust
- Response Validation
- Redundancy and Backup Systems
- Monitoring and Detection

```bash

claude-flow-complete_614.title: Start a Byzantine fault-tolerant swarm
claude-flow-complete_614.content:
claude-flow swarm "Critical system analysis" \
  --topology mesh \
  --byzantine-tolerance 3 \
  --consensus pbft \
  --trust-management enabled \
  --redundancy-factor 5
```

claude-flow-complete_629.content: claude-flow swarm execute "Build a web application with authentication"

claude-flow-complete_660.title: Development Phases
claude-flow-complete_660.content:
1. **Specification** - Define requirements, constraints, and success criteria
   - Key Modes: `researcher`, `analyzer`, `memory-manager`

2. **Architecture** - Design system structure and component interfaces
   - Key Modes: `architect`, `designer`, `orchestrator`

3. **Refinement (TDD)** - Implement features with test-first approach
   - Key Modes: `tdd`, `coder`, `tester`

4. **Review** - Ensure code quality, security, and performance
   - Key Modes: `reviewer`, `optimizer`, `debugger`

5. **Completion** - Integration, deployment, and monitoring
   - Key Modes: `workflow-manager`, `documenter`, `memory-manager`

claude-flow-complete_829.title: CRITICAL: CONCURRENT EXECUTION & FILE MANAGEMENT
claude-flow-complete_829.content:
**ABSOLUTE RULES**:
1. ALL operations MUST be concurrent/parallel in a single message
2. **NEVER save working files, text/mds and tests to the root folder**
3. ALWAYS organize files in appropriate subdirectories

claude-flow-complete_836.title: GOLDEN RULE: "1 MESSAGE = ALL RELATED OPERATIONS"
claude-flow-complete_836.content:
**MANDATORY PATTERNS:**
- **TodoWrite**: ALWAYS batch ALL todos in ONE call (5-10+ todos minimum)
- **Task tool**: ALWAYS spawn ALL agents in ONE message with full instructions
- **File operations**: ALWAYS batch ALL reads/writes/edits in ONE message
- **Bash commands**: ALWAYS batch ALL terminal operations in ONE message
- **Memory operations**: ALWAYS batch ALL memory store/retrieve in ONE message

claude-flow-complete_845.title: File Organization Rules
claude-flow-complete_845.content:
**NEVER save to root folder. Use these directories:**
- `/src` - Source code files
- `/tests` - Test files
- `/docs` - Documentation and markdown files
- `/config` - Configuration files
- `/scripts` - Utility scripts
- `/examples` - Example code
```

claude-flow-complete_919.title: v2.7.35 - 2025-11-13
claude-flow-complete_919.content:
**Automatic Error Recovery System** - Zero-intervention WSL error handling
- Automatic ENOTEMPTY npm cache error detection and cleanup
- WSL environment detection with proactive optimizations
- Intelligent retry logic with exponential backoff
- Up to 5 retry attempts with `--force` flag
- Automatic SQLite -> JSON fallback for database initialization
- 95%+ success rate on WSL (up from ~40%)

- Version negotiation with YYYY-MM format
- Async job management with job handles
- MCP Registry integration for server registration
- JSON Schema 1.1 validation (Draft 2020-12)
- 98.7% token reduction (150k->2k tokens)
- 10x faster startup (500-1000ms -> 50-100ms)
- 90% memory reduction (~50MB -> ~5MB)

**AgentDB v1.6.1**
- 150x faster vector search with HNSW indexing
- 56% memory reduction with optimized storage
- ReasoningBank integration for semantic memory

**Agentic-Flow v1.9.4**

- Circuit breaker patterns for cascading failure prevention
- Supabase cloud integration
- Checkpointing for crash recovery

- Fixed result mapping for `retrieveMemories()` flat structure
- Fixed parameter mismatch (namespace vs domain)

- Works without API keys (deterministic 1024-dim embeddings)

daa-complete_2.title: Executive Summary
daa-complete_2.content:
DAA is a production-ready Rust SDK for building quantum-resistant, self-managing AI agents with economic self-sufficiency. It provides a comprehensive framework for autonomous multi-agent coordination, distributed machine learning, and decentralized governance.

**Key Capabilities:**
- Quantum-resistant cryptography (ML-DSA, ML-KEM, HQC)
- MRAP Autonomy Loop (Monitor, Reason, Act, Reflect, Adapt)
- P2P networking via QuDAG protocol
- Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus
- Federated machine learning with Prime ML Framework
- Economic self-management with token-based incentives

---

daa-complete_18.title: 1.1 Core Crate Structure

daa/
├── daa-orchestrator/  # Agent lifecycle & workflow management

├── daa-rules/         # Governance rule engine

├── daa-compute/       # Distributed computation
├── daa-swarm/         # Multi-agent coordination
├── prime-ml/          # Core ML framework
├── prime-distributed/ # Distributed training
├── prime-memory/      # Gradient memory
└── qudag/             # P2P network infrastructure
```

daa-complete_69.title: 2.1 MRAP Autonomy Loop
daa-complete_69.content:
The core decision-making cycle for autonomous agents:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 MRAP LOOP                     │
│                                              │
│   ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌───────┐ │
│   │ Monitor │ ──▶ │ Reason  │ ──▶ │  Act  │ │
│   └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └───────┘ │
│        ▲                               │     │
│        │         ┌─────────┐          │     │
│        └──────── │  Adapt  │ ◀────────┘     │
│                  └─────────┘                 │
│                       ▲                      │
│                  ┌─────────┐                 │
│                  │ Reflect │                 │
│                  └─────────┘                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Loop Phases:**
1. **Monitor**: Observe environment, collect metrics, detect events
2. **Reason**: Analyze data, evaluate options, make decisions
3. **Act**: Execute actions, interact with other agents/services
4. **Reflect**: Evaluate outcomes, identify improvements
5. **Adapt**: Update strategies, learn from experience

daa-complete_345.title: 4.2 Kademlia DHT
daa-complete_345.content:
Distributed hash table for peer and content discovery:

```rust
pub struct KademliaConfig {
    // Standard parameters
    replication_factor: NonZeroUsize,  // k=20
    query_parallelism: NonZeroUsize,   // alpha=3
    record_ttl: Duration,              // 24 hours

    // QuDAG enhancements
    dark_addressing_support: bool,     // Shadow address lookups
    quantum_resistant_ids: bool,       // ML-DSA node IDs
    reputation_weighted_routing: bool, // Prefer high-rep peers
}
```

**DHT Operations:**
1. **Peer Discovery**: Bootstrap, recursive lookups, reputation weighting
2. **Content Routing**: DAG vertex locations, provider records
3. **Network Maintenance**: Bucket refresh, stale peer eviction

daa-complete_368.title: 4.3 Gossipsub Protocol
daa-complete_368.content:
Pub/sub messaging with mesh networking:

```rust
pub struct GossipsubConfig {
    mesh_n: usize,           // Target mesh size (6)
    mesh_n_low: usize,       // Minimum mesh size (4)
    mesh_n_high: usize,      // Maximum mesh size (12)
    gossip_factor: f64,      // Gossip to D_lazy peers (0.25)
    heartbeat_interval: Duration,
}

// Topic-based message routing
pub enum Topic {
    Consensus,      // Consensus messages (high priority)
    Transactions,   // Transaction propagation
    StateSync,      // State synchronization
    AgentComm,      // Agent-to-agent communication
}
```

daa-complete_684.title: 6.2 Security Audit Summary (November 2025)
daa-complete_684.content:
**Overall Rating: A- (85/100)**

| Category | Score | Status |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Cryptographic Implementation | 88/100 | Strong |
| Memory Safety | 92/100 | Excellent |
| Input Validation | 75/100 | Good |
| Dependency Security | 90/100 | Strong |
| Code Quality | 80/100 | Good |

**High Priority Issues:**
1. **H-1**: Placeholder crypto in some modules (use real ML-KEM/ML-DSA)
2. **H-2**: Command injection risk in shell execution paths
3. **H-3**: Unsafe SIMD code requires thorough review

**Recommendations:**
- Replace 5,473 `unwrap()` calls with proper error handling
- Implement rate limiting on all network endpoints
- Add comprehensive fuzzing for crypto primitives

daa-complete_868.title: 10.1 Golden Rule
daa-complete_868.content:
**"1 MESSAGE = ALL RELATED OPERATIONS"**

All operations must be concurrent/parallel in a single message:
- TodoWrite: Batch ALL todos in ONE call
- Task tool: Spawn ALL agents in ONE message
- File operations: Batch ALL reads/writes/edits
- Bash commands: Batch ALL terminal operations
- Memory operations: Batch ALL memory store/retrieve

daa-complete_879.title: 10.2 Agent Coordination Protocol
daa-complete_879.content:

```bash
npx claude-flow@alpha hooks pre-task --description "[task]"
npx claude-flow@alpha hooks session-restore --session-id "swarm-[id]"
```

**During Work:**
```bash
npx claude-flow@alpha hooks post-edit --file "[file]" --memory-key "swarm/[agent]/[step]"
npx claude-flow@alpha hooks notify --message "[what was done]"
```

**After Work:**
```bash
npx claude-flow@alpha hooks post-task --task-id "[task]"
npx claude-flow@alpha hooks session-end --export-metrics true
```

decision-frameworks_2.title: Executive Summary
decision-frameworks_2.content:
This document provides definitive guidance for selecting the right tools from the ruvnet ecosystem for any agentic AI use case. The ecosystem comprises 160+ repositories with overlapping capabilities - this framework eliminates confusion and provides clear decision paths.

---

decision-frameworks_10.title: 1.1 Orchestration Framework Selection
decision-frameworks_10.content:
| Need | Use This | Not This | Why |
|------|----------|----------|-----|
| **Local development with Claude Code** | claude-flow | agentic-flow | Claude-flow has direct MCP integration with 100+ tools, hooks system, and SPARC methodology built-in |
| **Production deployments at scale** | agentic-flow | claude-flow | Agentic-flow has Agent Booster (352x faster), ReasoningBank, billing system, and Kubernetes GitOps controller |
| **Cloud sandboxes and gamified learning** | flow-nexus | claude-flow | Flow-nexus provides E2B sandboxes, credit-based economy, challenges, and cloud infrastructure |
| **Simple single-agent tasks** | agentic-flow CLI | swarm orchestration | Direct agent execution without coordination overhead |
| **Complex multi-agent coordination** | claude-flow hive-mind | simple swarm | Queen-led coordination with persistent memory and session management |
| **Ephemeral agents (5s-15min lifetime)** | agentic-flow Federation Hub | static agents | Purpose-built for short-lived specialized agents with persistent cross-agent memory |

decision-frameworks_21.title: 1.2 Vector Database Selection
decision-frameworks_21.content:
| Need | Use This | Not This | Why |
|------|----------|----------|-----|
| **High-performance production vector search** | ruvector | ChromaDB/Pinecone | 61us latency (vs 50ms-2ms), GNN self-learning, Cypher graph queries, 2-32x compression |
| **PostgreSQL integration** | ruvector-postgres | pgvector | 77+ SQL functions, SIMD acceleration, SPARQL support, local embeddings |
| **Browser/edge deployment** | rvLite or ruvector-wasm | server-based DB | Full WASM support, SQL+SPARQL+Cypher unified interface |
| **Agent memory with learning** | AgentDB (via agentic-flow) | raw vector store | 96x-164x faster, reflexion memory, skill library, 9 RL algorithms |
| **Simple embedding storage** | AgentDB or ReasoningBank | full ruvector | Lower complexity for basic use cases |

decision-frameworks_31.title: 1.3 Learning and Adaptation Selection
decision-frameworks_31.content:
| Need | Use This | Not This | Why |
|------|----------|----------|-----|
| **Agent self-improvement over time** | SAFLA | manual training | Persistent memory, self-learning loop, meta-cognitive engine, 172k+ ops/sec |
| **Semantic pattern learning** | ReasoningBank (agentic-flow) | static memory | Pattern matching, trajectory storage, 46% faster execution over time |
| **Neural network self-learning** | SONA (ruvector) | external ML | Two-tier LoRA, EWC++ (prevents catastrophic forgetting), sub-millisecond learning |
| **Distributed ML training** | DAA Prime framework | single-node training | Byzantine fault tolerance, gradient aggregation, federated learning |
| **Quick experiments** | SAFLA MCP integration | custom implementation | One-command setup: `claude mcp add safla python3 safla_mcp_enhanced.py` |

decision-frameworks_41.title: 1.4 Agent Architecture Selection
decision-frameworks_41.content:
| Need | Use This | Not This | Why |
|------|----------|----------|-----|
| **Decentralized autonomous agents** | DAA SDK | centralized orchestration | Quantum-resistant security, economic self-sufficiency, P2P networking |
| **Swarm intelligence** | claude-flow swarm | single agents | Mesh/hierarchical/ring/star topologies, consensus mechanisms |
| **P2P neural mesh** | Synaptic-Mesh | server-based | Distributed micro-neural networks (1K-100K params each), edge computing |
| **Enterprise orchestration** | claude-flow + SPARC | ad-hoc development | Structured methodology with 54 specialized agents |
| **Quick prototyping** | agentic-flow CLI | full swarm setup | Single command execution with 150+ agents available |

decision-frameworks_51.title: 1.5 Communication and Transport
decision-frameworks_51.content:
| Need | Use This | Not This | Why |
|------|----------|----------|-----|
| **Ultra-low latency agent communication** | QUIC Transport (agentic-flow) | TCP/HTTP | 50-70% faster, 0-RTT reconnection, survives network changes |
| **MCP-based tool integration** | claude-flow MCP server | custom APIs | 100+ tools, standardized protocol, Claude Code integration |
| **Cloud-based MCP** | flow-nexus MCP server | local MCP | 70+ cloud tools, E2B sandboxes, distributed training |
| **Quantum-resistant networking** | QuDAG | standard TLS | Post-quantum crypto (ML-DSA, ML-KEM, HQC), DAG consensus |

---

decision-frameworks_64.title: 2.1 Single Agent vs Multi-Agent Decision Flow
decision-frameworks_64.content:
```
START: What is your task?
    |
    +---> Simple, well-defined task (< 1 hour)?
    |         |
    |         YES --> Use single agent (agentic-flow CLI)
    |                    npx agentic-flow --agent coder --task "..."
    |
    +---> Complex task requiring multiple perspectives?
    |         |
    |         +---> Need real-time coordination?
    |         |         |
    |         |         YES --> claude-flow swarm (mesh topology)
    |         |                    npx claude-flow swarm init --topology mesh
    |         |
    |         +---> Need hierarchical control?
    |         |         |
    |         |         YES --> claude-flow hive-mind (queen-led)
    |         |                    npx claude-flow hive-mind spawn "task"
    |         |
    |         +---> Need autonomous operation?
    |                   |
    |                   YES --> DAA SDK (decentralized)
    |                              Use daa-orchestrator with rules engine
    |
    +---> Ephemeral specialized tasks?
              |
              YES --> agentic-flow Federation Hub
                         npx agentic-flow federation spawn
```

decision-frameworks_97.title: 2.2 Local vs Distributed Decision Flow
decision-frameworks_97.content:
```
START: What are your deployment requirements?
    |
    +---> Development/testing?
    |         |
    |         YES --> Local execution
    |                    claude-flow or agentic-flow CLI
    |                    All 213 MCP tools work
    |
    +---> Production with scale requirements?
    |         |
    |         +---> Need cloud sandboxes?
    |         |         |
    |         |         YES --> flow-nexus (E2B integration)
    |         |
    |         +---> Need container orchestration?
    |         |         |
    |         |         YES --> agentic-flow K8s GitOps Controller
    |         |                    helm install agentic-jujutsu
    |         |
    |         +---> Need distributed ML?
    |                   |
    |                   YES --> DAA Prime framework or flow-nexus neural clusters
    |
    +---> Edge deployment?
    |         |
    |         YES --> ruvector-wasm, rvLite, or Synaptic-Mesh
    |                    Runs in browser, edge devices, IoT
    |
    +---> Privacy-sensitive (offline required)?
              |
              YES --> ONNX local inference (agentic-flow)
                         100% free, no API needed, 6+ tokens/sec CPU
```

decision-frameworks_134.title: 2.3 Sync vs Async Decision Flow
decision-frameworks_134.content:
```
START: What is your latency requirement?
    |
    +---> Real-time response needed (< 100ms)?
    |         |
    |         +---> High-frequency agent coordination?
    |         |         |
    |         |         YES --> QUIC Transport (agentic-flow)
    |         |                    50-70% faster than TCP
    |         |
    |         +---> Vector search?
    |         |         |
    |         |         YES --> ruvector with SIMD
    |         |                    61us latency, 16,400 QPS
    |         |
    |         +---> Memory operations?
    |                   |
    |                   YES --> AgentDB (96x faster) or ReasoningBank (2-3ms)
    |
    +---> Batch processing acceptable?
    |         |
    |         YES --> agentic-flow batch_process
    |                    172k+ ops/sec, parallel execution
    |
    +---> Long-running distributed task?
              |
              +---> Need fault tolerance?
              |         |
              |         YES --> DAA with Byzantine consensus
              |
              +---> Need workflow orchestration?
                        |
                        YES --> flow-nexus workflows
                                   Event-driven, message queues, auto-retry
```

---

decision-frameworks_176.title: 3.1 Small Project (1-5 developers, prototype/MVP)
decision-frameworks_176.content:
**Recommended Stack:**
```
Core:           agentic-flow (CLI + Agent Booster)
Memory:         ReasoningBank (built-in, no setup)
Vector Search:  AgentDB (included with agentic-flow)
MCP Server:     claude-flow MCP (100 tools)
```

**Setup Commands:**
```bash

decision-frameworks_188.title: Install
decision-frameworks_188.content:
npm install -g agentic-flow
npm install -g claude-flow@alpha

decision-frameworks_192.title: Add MCP server
decision-frameworks_192.content: claude mcp add claude-flow npx claude-flow@alpha mcp start

decision-frameworks_195.title: Run agents
decision-frameworks_195.content:
npx agentic-flow --agent coder --task "Build REST API" --optimize
```

**Why This Stack:**
- Zero configuration required
- Agent Booster gives 352x performance boost automatically
- ReasoningBank learns from interactions without setup
- Single install gives access to 150+ agents
- Cost: $0 with local ONNX, or optimized via multi-model router

decision-frameworks_206.title: 3.2 Medium Project (5-20 developers, production application)
decision-frameworks_206.content:
**Recommended Stack:**
```
Orchestration:  claude-flow hive-mind + agentic-flow
Memory:         AgentDB v2 (RuVector-powered)
Vector Search:  ruvector (150x faster than SQLite)
Learning:       SAFLA (self-improving agents)
MCP Servers:    claude-flow + ruv-swarm
Infrastructure: Docker containers
```

**Setup Commands:**
```bash

decision-frameworks_220.title: Core tools
decision-frameworks_220.content:
npm install -g claude-flow@alpha agentic-flow
pip install safla

decision-frameworks_224.title: MCP servers
decision-frameworks_224.content:
claude mcp add claude-flow npx claude-flow@alpha mcp start
claude mcp add ruv-swarm npx ruv-swarm mcp start
claude mcp add safla python3 safla_mcp_enhanced.py

decision-frameworks_229.title: Initialize project

decision-frameworks_294.title: 4.1 Speed vs Accuracy
decision-frameworks_294.content:
| Priority | Tool Choice | Trade-off |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Maximum Speed** | Agent Booster + QUIC | 352x faster code edits, 50-70% lower latency. May skip some validation. |
| **Maximum Accuracy** | Full swarm + SAFLA learning | Slower execution but 90%+ success rate, learns from mistakes |
| **Balanced** | ReasoningBank + AgentDB | 2-3ms queries with 46% accuracy improvement over time |

**Decision Matrix:**
```
Speed Critical (real-time):     Agent Booster + QUIC + ruvector SIMD
Accuracy Critical (production): SAFLA + full validation + rollback capability
Cost Critical:                  Local ONNX (free) or DeepSeek R1 (85% cheaper)
All balanced:                   agentic-flow with --optimize flag
```

decision-frameworks_310.title: 4.2 Memory vs Compute
decision-frameworks_310.content:
| Constraint | Tool Choice | Trade-off |
|------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Limited Memory (edge/browser)** | rvLite + ruvector-wasm | 2-32x compression, runs in 50MB |
| **Limited Compute (CPU only)** | ONNX Phi-4 local | ~6 tokens/sec, no GPU needed |
| **Unlimited Resources** | Full ruvector + DAA cluster | Maximum performance, horizontal scaling |

**Compression Options (ruvector):**
| Tier | Format | Compression | Latency Trade-off |
|------|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Hot | f32 | 1x | None (full precision) |
| Warm | f16 | 2x | Imperceptible |
| Cool | PQ8 | 8x | ~1ms overhead |
| Cold | PQ4 | 16x | Still fast |
| Archive | Binary | 32x | Batch retrieval |

decision-frameworks_327.title: 4.3 Complexity vs Maintainability
decision-frameworks_327.content:
| Project Stage | Recommended Complexity | Tools |
|---------------|------------------------|-------|
| **Prototype** | Minimal | agentic-flow CLI only |
| **MVP** | Low | + ReasoningBank memory |
| **Production v1** | Medium | + claude-flow + SAFLA |
| **Scale** | High | + flow-nexus + DAA + K8s |
| **Enterprise** | Full | All ecosystem tools |

**Maintainability Guidelines:**
- Start simple, add complexity only when needed
- Use MCP standard for all tool integration (future-proof)
- Prefer declarative configuration (YAML) over imperative code
- Use hooks system for cross-cutting concerns
- Document agent decisions in ReasoningBank for debugging

---

dspy-ts-complete_2.title: Overview
dspy-ts-complete_2.content:
**DSPy.ts** is a TypeScript implementation of Stanford's DSPy framework - a paradigm shift from "prompting" to "programming" AI systems. Instead of manually crafting prompts, DSPy.ts lets you define modular, composable programs that can be automatically optimized.

**Core Philosophy**: "Program AI Systems, Don't Prompt Them"

**Repository**: `/tmp/ruvnet-repos/dspy.ts/`
**Version**: 2.0.0
**License**: MIT

---

dspy-ts-complete_62.content:
| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Signatures** | Type-safe specifications for module inputs/outputs |
| **Modules** | Composable units (Predict, ChainOfThought, ReAct) |
| **Optimizers** | Automatic prompt/weight optimization |
| **Pipelines** | Chain modules with retry logic |
| **AgentDB** | Vector database (150x faster than baseline) |
| **ReasoningBank** | Self-learning memory with SAFLA algorithm |
| **Swarm** | Multi-agent orchestration |

---

dspy-ts-complete_78.title: The DSPy Paradigm
dspy-ts-complete_78.content:
Traditional approach:
```
User -> Prompt Engineering -> LLM -> Output
```

DSPy approach:
```
User -> Define Signature -> Create Module -> Optimize -> Deploy
```

dspy-ts-complete_90.title: Benefits
dspy-ts-complete_90.content:
1. **Declarative**: Define what you want, not how to get it
2. **Composable**: Chain modules together
3. **Optimizable**: Automatic improvement through training
4. **Type-Safe**: Full TypeScript support
5. **Testable**: Unit test your AI programs

dspy-ts-complete_1296.title: Summary
dspy-ts-complete_1296.content:
DSPy.ts 2.0 provides a complete TypeScript framework for building declarative, self-improving AI programs:

1. **Signatures** define type-safe I/O contracts
2. **Modules** (Predict, ChainOfThought, ReAct) implement reasoning patterns
3. **Optimizers** (BootstrapFewShot) automatically improve performance
4. **Pipelines** chain modules with retry logic
5. **AgentDB** provides 150x faster vector search
6. **ReasoningBank** enables self-learning with SAFLA
7. **Swarm** orchestrates multi-agent coordination

The framework is production-ready for core use cases and actively being extended with advanced optimizers and modules.

---

gpt-ecosystem_2.title: Executive Summary
gpt-ecosystem_2.content:
This document provides guru-level documentation for the complete RuvNet GPT ecosystem, covering 17+ repositories focused on ChatGPT plugins, bots, prompt engineering, and AI-powered tools. The ecosystem spans:

- **ChatGPT Plugins**: Infrastructure for extending ChatGPT with external APIs
- **Bot Generators**: Meta-tools for creating specialized AI assistants
- **Prompt Engineering**: Advanced frameworks for prompt optimization
- **Domain-Specific GPTs**: Specialized assistants for trading, AWS, blockchain, and more
- **Prompt Languages**: Novel DSLs for AI interaction optimization

ChatGPT Plug-in Creator

You are ChatGPT Plug-in manifest creation tool! This tool will help you
convert CURL commands into the YAML format for a ChatGPT Plug-in manifest.

gpt-ecosystem_46.title: Manifest Schema Fields
gpt-ecosystem_46.content:

| schema_version       | String        | Manifest schema version                  |

| name_for_human       | String        | Human-readable name                      |
| description_for_model| String        | Description for model context            |
| description_for_human| String        | Human-readable description               |
| auth                 | ManifestAuth  | Authentication schema                    |
| api                  | Object        | API specification                        |
| logo_url             | String        | URL for plugin logo                      |
| contact_email        | String        | Support/moderation email                 |
| legal_info_url       | String        | Legal information URL                    |
```

gpt-ecosystem_113.title: AutoGPT YAML Template Structure
gpt-ecosystem_113.content:
```yaml
ai_goals:
  - 'Understand and analyze text documents, articles, and web pages'
  - 'Generate concise summaries of the provided content'
  - 'Maintain the most important information and key points'
  - 'Ensure readability and grammatical correctness'
  - 'Adapt to various document styles and formats'
  - 'Handle different levels of summarization'
  - 'Provide feedback and suggestions for improving content'
  - 'Continuously learn and improve based on user feedback'
  - 'Visit AutoGPT GitHub for more instructions'

ai_name: 'SummarizeBot'
ai_role: 'Content Summarization Assistant'
```

gpt-ecosystem_131.title: Midjourney Bot Prompt Structure
gpt-ecosystem_131.content:
```text
Layer-Separated Prompt Guidelines:
1. Break description into multiple layers
2. Assign weights (::X where X is importance number)
3. Use negative weights to negate unwanted aspects
4. Keep tokens in layers congruous
5. Be descriptive with nouns and visual phrases
6. Stay within 250 token limit

Example:
* Anthropomorphic fox person ::8
* Cute, friendly smile, bushy tail ::9
* Colorful outfit, overalls ::4
* Holding a balloon, floating ::3
* Watercolor illustration ::2
* No castle ::-1
```

gpt-ecosystem_440.title: Core Conversion Function
gpt-ecosystem_440.content:
```python
def convert_swagger_to_chatgpt_manifest(swagger_yaml, name, description,
                                        base_url, spec_file_name,
                                        user_authenticated, logo_url,
                                        contact_email, legal_info_url):
    # Extract info from Swagger
    info = swagger_yaml.get('info', {})
    title = info.get('title', 'My API Plugin')
    description = info.get('description', 'Plugin for API.')

    # Create ChatGPT manifest
    manifest = {
        'schema_version': 'v1',
        'name_for_human': name or title,
        'description_for_human': description,
        'description_for_model': description,
        'auth': {'type': 'none'},
        'api': {
            'type': 'openapi',
            'url': f"{base_url}/plugins/{spec_file_name}",
            'is_user_authenticated': user_authenticated
        },
        'logo_url': logo_url,
        'contact_email': contact_email,
        'legal_info_url': legal_info_url
    }

    # Update endpoints with auth
    paths = swagger_yaml.get('paths', {})
    for path_key, path_item in paths.items():
        for method_key, method_item in path_item.items():
            if method_key.lower() == 'post':
                method_item['x-auth-type'] = 'none' if not user_authenticated else 'apiKey'
                method_item['x-auth-location'] = 'query'

    return yaml.dump(manifest), yaml.dump(swagger_yaml)
```

**Repository**: `ChatGPT-Trading-Bot-for-KuCoin`

gpt-ecosystem_744.title: Trading Logic
gpt-ecosystem_744.content:
```python
import ccxt
import numpy as np
import openai

def compute_rsi(data, period):
    """Calculate RSI without external libraries."""
    delta = np.diff(data)
    gain, loss = delta.copy(), delta.copy()
    gain[gain < 0] = 0
    loss[loss > 0] = 0
    avg_gain = np.average(gain[-period:])
    avg_loss = -np.average(loss[-period:])
    rs = avg_gain / avg_loss
    rsi = 100 - (100 / (1 + rs))
    return rsi

def gpt_up_down(data):
    """Get GPT prediction for market direction."""
    preprompt = """
    say up or down for the next day in the time series
    that hasn't happened. ONLY say one single word,
    UP or DOWN. Don't explain anything.
    """
    completions = openai.ChatCompletion.create(
        model="gpt-3.5-turbo",
        max_tokens=5,
        temperature=0.2,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": preprompt},
            {"role": "user", "content": str(data)}
        ]
    )
    return completions.choices[0].text.strip()

def trade_logic():
    """Main trading decision logic."""
    ticker = exchange.fetch_ticker(symbol)
    midpoint = (ticker['bid'] + ticker['ask']) / 2

    data = exchange.fetch_ohlcv(symbol, '1h', limit=30)
    rsi = calculate_rsi(data, rsi_period)
    gpt_prediction = gpt_up_down(data)

    # Buy condition: RSI oversold + GPT predicts UP
    if rsi <= rsi_oversold and gpt_prediction == 'up':
        exchange.create_market_order(symbol, 'buy', amount)

    # Sell condition: RSI overbought + GPT predicts DOWN
    elif rsi >= rsi_overbought and gpt_prediction == 'down':
        exchange.create_market_order(symbol, 'sell', btc_balance)
```

| Set Theory | Component combination | `↹ components•sets` `⊕ combine => union` `Σ result + validation` |
| Category Theory | Structure transformations | `↹ source•target•mapping` `⊕ preserve => properties` `Σ transformed + verified` |
| Topology | Continuous transformations | `↹ system•changes•boundaries` `⊕ maintain => continuity` `Σ robust + stable` |
| Abstract Algebra | Operation composition | `↹ operations•elements` `⊕ compose => structure` `Σ result + properties` |

gpt-ecosystem_1373.title: Graph of Thoughts (GoT) Problem Solving
gpt-ecosystem_1373.content:
```text
Task: Sort a list too long for standard LLM methods

GoT Instructions:
1. Initial Split: Divide list into smaller subarrays
2. Individual Sorting: Sort each subarray independently
3. Intermediate Thoughts: Each sorted subarray = thought
4. Thought Aggregation: Merge subarrays using graph structure
5. Final Output: Fully sorted list

Model as graph:
- Vertices: Steps in sorting process (thoughts)
- Edges: Dependencies (splitting/merging)
- Navigate graph to reach final sorted list
```

gpt-ecosystem_1652.title: A.1 Complete Plugin Example
gpt-ecosystem_1652.content:
```python
"""
Complete ChatGPT Plugin Example
"""
import os
import json
from quart import Quart, request, jsonify
from quart_cors import cors

app = Quart(__name__)
app = cors(app, allow_origin="https://chat.openai.com")

SERVICE_AUTH_KEY = os.environ.get("SERVICE_AUTH_KEY")
DATA_STORE = {}

@app.before_request
async def auth_required():
    """Verify authentication for all requests."""
    # Skip auth for manifest and spec
    if request.path in ['/.well-known/ai-plugin.json', '/openapi.yaml']:
        return None

    auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")
    if not auth_header or auth_header != f"Bearer {SERVICE_AUTH_KEY}":
        return jsonify({"error": "Unauthorized"}), 401

@app.route("/.well-known/ai-plugin.json")
async def plugin_manifest():
    """Serve plugin manifest."""
    host = request.headers['Host']
    return jsonify({
        "schema_version": "v1",
        "name_for_human": "My Plugin",
        "name_for_model": "my_plugin",
        "description_for_human": "A sample ChatGPT plugin",
        "description_for_model": "A sample ChatGPT plugin for demonstration",
        "auth": {
            "type": "service_http",
            "authorization_type": "bearer",
            "verification_tokens": {
                "openai": SERVICE_AUTH_KEY
            }
        },
        "api": {
            "type": "openapi",
            "url": f"https://{host}/openapi.yaml"
        },
        "logo_url": f"https://{host}/logo.png",
        "contact_email": "support@example.com",
        "legal_info_url": "https://example.com/legal"
    })

@app.route("/openapi.yaml")
async def openapi_spec():
    """Serve OpenAPI specification."""
    host = request.headers['Host']
    return f"""
openapi: 3.0.1
info:
  title: My Plugin API
  description: A sample ChatGPT plugin API
  version: 'v1'
servers:
  - url: https://{host}
paths:
  /data:
    get:
      operationId: getData
      summary: Get all data
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Suc

gpt-ecosystem_1891.title: Conclusion
gpt-ecosystem_1891.content:
The RuvNet GPT ecosystem represents a comprehensive toolkit for extending, optimizing, and building upon ChatGPT and related AI systems. Key takeaways:

1. **Plugin Development**: Use the provided templates and patterns to create ChatGPT plugins that extend capabilities with external APIs
2. **Prompt Engineering**: Leverage frameworks like Prompt Engine, PromptLang, and SynthLang to create optimized, reusable prompts
3. **Domain Integration**: Use specialized plugins (AWS, Tatum, KuCoin) as templates for creating domain-specific integrations
4. **Meta-Tooling**: The Bot-Generator-Bot pattern enables rapid creation of specialized assistants without coding

The ecosystem continues to evolve with new frameworks, optimizations, and integrations being added regularly.

integration-patterns_155.content:
import asyncio
import numpy as np
from safla import HybridMemoryArchitecture, SAFLAConfig, get_logger
from safla.core.safety_validation import OptimizedSafetyValidator
from safla.core.delta_evaluation import OptimizedDeltaEvaluator

async def setup_safla():
    """Initialize SAFLA with hybrid memory and safety validation."""

    # Step 1: Configuration
    config = SAFLAConfig()
    config.debug = True
    config.memory.max_memories = 1000
    config.safety.memory_limit = 100_000_000  # 100MB

    # Step 2: Initialize core components
    memory = HybridMemoryArchitecture()
    safety = OptimizedSafetyValidator()
    evaluator = OptimizedDeltaEvaluator()

    print("SAFLA Core Components Initialized:")
    print("  - Hybrid Memory: Vector + Episodic + Semantic + Working")
    print("  - Safety Validation: Constraint checking + Risk assessment")
    print("  - Delta Evaluation: Performance improvement tracking")

    # Step 3: Store memories with embeddings
    test_embedding = np.random.randn(512).astype(np.float32)
    memory_id = memory.vector_memory.store(
        embedding=test_embedding,
        metadata={
            "type": "knowledge",
            "content": "Important domain knowledge",
            "timestamp": "now"
        }
    )
    print(f"Stored memory: {memory_id}")

    # Step 4: Validate safety constraints
    result = await safety.validate(
        "new operation request",
        context={"operation": "data_access", "sensitivity": "low"}
    )
    print(f"Safety validation: {'PASSED' if result.is_safe else 'FAILED'}")

    # Step 5: Evaluate performance delta
    delta = evaluator.evaluate_delta(
        performance_data={
            "current_reward": 1.0,
            "previous_reward": 0.9,
            "tokens_used": 100
        }
    )
    print(f"Performance delta: {delta.overall_delta:.3f}")

    return memory, safety, evaluator

import asyncio
from safla import HybridMemoryArchitecture, SAFLAConfig
from safla.core.safety_validation import OptimizedSafetyValidator
from safla.core.delta_evaluation import OptimizedDeltaEvaluator
import numpy as np


async def safla_rag():
    # Step 1: Initialize SAFLA components
    config = SAFLAConfig()
    memory = HybridMemoryArchitecture()
    safety = OptimizedSafetyValidator()
    evaluator = OptimizedDeltaEvaluator()

    # Step 2: Ingest documents into hybrid memory
    for doc in documents:
        # Vector memory for similarity search
        embedding = generate_embedding(doc.content)  # Use your preferred model
        memory.vector_memory.store(
            embedding=embedding,
            metadata={
                "content": doc.content,
                "doc_id": doc.id,
                "type": "document"
            }
        )

        # Semantic memory for knowledge graph
        memory.semantic_memory.add_concept(
            concept=doc.title,
            attributes={"content": doc.content}
        )

        # Episodic memory for temporal tracking
        memory.episodic_memory.store_event(
            event_type="document_ingested",
            context={"doc_id": doc.id}
        )

    # Step 3: Query with safety validation
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(question)

    # Safety check before retrieval
    safety_result = await safety.validate(
        question,
        context={"operation": "retrieval"}
    )

    if not safety_result.is_safe:
        raise ValueError(f"Query blocked: {safety_result.violations}")

    # Step 4: Multi-modal retrieval
    # Vector similarity search
    vector_results = memory.vector_memory.similarity_search(
        query_embedding=query_embedding,
        k=5
    )

    # Semantic graph traversal
    semantic_results = memory.semantic_memory.query_related(
        concept=extract_main_concept(question),
        depth=2
    )

    # Combine results with working memory context

integration-patterns_3520.title: Summary
integration-patterns_3520.content:
This integration patterns guide covers:

1. **Quick Start** - Minimal setup for each component
2. **Common Patterns** - Vector memory, swarm coordination, self-learning, streaming
3. **Full Stack Examples** - Complete RAG, autonomous agents, production swarms
4. **Anti-Patterns** - What to avoid and common mistakes
5. **Migration Patterns** - Moving from other frameworks
6. **Advanced Patterns** - Federated learning, hierarchical memory, self-healing

Key takeaways:
- Use WASM when available (10x performance boost)
- Implement proper error handling and recovery
- Cache embeddings to reduce computation
- Use appropriate memory tiers for different access patterns
- Learn from experience with ReasoningBank
- Validate safety before critical operations

For more examples, see the individual repository READMEs:
- [ruvector](https://github.com/ruvnet/ruvector)
- [claude-flow](https://github.com/ruvnet/claude-flow)
- [agentic-flow](https://github.com/ruvnet/agentic-flow)
- [SAFLA](https://github.com/ruvnet/SAFLA)

ruvector-complete_8.title: Overview
ruvector-complete_8.content: **RuVector is a distributed vector database that learns.** It combines vector storage, graph queries with Cypher, horizontal scaling with Raft consensus, and self-improving search through Graph Neural Networks (GNN).

ruvector-complete_12.title: Core Value Proposition
ruvector-complete_12.content:
Think of RuVector as: **Pinecone + Neo4j + PyTorch + postgres + etcd** in one Rust package.

```bash
npx ruvector
```

ruvector-complete_20.title: What Makes RuVector Different
ruvector-complete_20.content:
Traditional vector databases just store and search. RuVector:

1. **Stores vectors** like any vector DB (embeddings from OpenAI, Cohere, etc.)
2. **Queries with Cypher** like Neo4j (`MATCH (a)-[:SIMILAR]->(b) RETURN b`)
3. **The index learns** - GNN layers make search results improve over time
4. **Scales horizontally** - Raft consensus, multi-master replication, auto-sharding
5. **Routes AI requests** - Semantic routing and FastGRNN neural inference for LLM optimization
6. **Compresses automatically** - 2-32x memory reduction with adaptive tiered compression
7. **39 attention mechanisms** - Flash, linear, graph, hyperbolic for custom models
8. **Drops into Postgres** - pgvector-compatible extension with SIMD acceleration
9. **Runs anywhere** - Node.js, browser (WASM), HTTP server, or native Rust
10. **Continuous learning** - SONA enables runtime adaptation with LoRA, EWC++, and ReasoningBank

ruvector-complete_105.title: Vector Search
ruvector-complete_105.content:
| Feature | What It Does | Why It Matters |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| **HNSW Indexing** | O(log n) approximate nearest neighbor search | Fast enough for real-time apps |
| **Multiple Distance Metrics** | Euclidean, Cosine, Dot Product, Manhattan | Choose best for your embeddings |
| **Advanced Quantization** | Scalar (4x), Product (8-32x), Binary (32x) | Memory-efficient storage |
| **SIMD Optimizations** | Hardware-accelerated via SimSIMD | 200-300x speedup |
| **Zero-Copy I/O** | Memory-mapped storage | Instant loading |
| **Metadata Filtering** | Filter vectors by properties | Combine semantic + structured search |
| **Collections** | Namespace isolation, multi-tenancy | Organize by project/user |

ruvector-complete_117.title: Graph Neural Networks
ruvector-complete_117.content:
Traditional vector search:
```
Query -> HNSW Index -> Top K Results
```

RuVector with GNN:
```
Query -> HNSW Index -> GNN Layer -> Enhanced Results
              ^                          |
              +------ learns from -------+
```

The GNN layer:
1. Takes your query and its nearest neighbors
2. Applies multi-head attention to weigh which neighbors matter
3. Updates representations based on graph structure
4. Returns better-ranked results

Over time, frequently-accessed paths get reinforced, making common queries faster and more accurate.

ruvector-complete_139.title: Cypher Query Language
ruvector-complete_139.content:
RuVector implements Neo4j-compatible Cypher with hyperedge extensions:

```cypher
-- Create nodes
CREATE (p:Person {name: 'Alice', age: 30})

-- Create relationships
CREATE (a:Person {name: 'Alice'})-[:KNOWS]->(b:Person {name: 'Bob'})

-- Pattern matching
MATCH (p:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(friend)
WHERE p.name = 'Alice'
RETURN friend.name

-- Hyperedges (N-ary relationships)
CREATE (a:Person)-[:ATTENDED]->(meeting:Meeting, room:Room, company:Company)
```

**Supported Clauses:** MATCH, CREATE, MERGE, SET, DELETE, RETURN, WITH, WHERE, ORDER BY, SKIP, LIMIT

**Operators:** Arithmetic (+, -, *, /, %, ^), Comparison (=, <>, <, >, <=, >=), Logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR), String (STARTS WITH, ENDS WITH, CONTAINS)

**Aggregations:** COUNT, AVG, MIN, MAX, SUM, COLLECT

ruvector-complete_165.title: Distributed Systems (Raft Consensus)
ruvector-complete_165.content:
| Feature | What It Does | Why It Matters |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| **Raft Consensus** | Leader election, log replication | Strong consistency for metadata |
| **Auto-Sharding** | Consistent hashing, shard migration | Scale to billions of vectors |
| **Multi-Master Replication** | Write to any node, conflict resolution | High availability, no SPOF |
| **Snapshots** | Point-in-time backups, incremental | Disaster recovery |
| **Cluster Metrics** | Prometheus-compatible monitoring | Observability at scale |

**Raft Configuration Example:**
```rust
let config = RaftNodeConfig {
    node_id: "node-1".into(),
    cluster_members: vec!["node-1", "node-2", "node-3", "node-4", "node-5"]
        .into_iter().map(Into::into).collect(),
    election_timeout_min: 150,  // ms
    election_timeout_max: 300,  // ms
    heartbeat_interval: 50,     // ms
};
let raft = RaftNode::new(config);
```

ruvector-complete_215.title: Compression Tiers
ruvector-complete_215.content:
The architecture adapts to your data automatically. Hot paths get full precision; cold paths compress automatically.

| Access Frequency | Format | Compression | What Happens |
|-----------------|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Hot** (>80%) | f32 | 1x | Full precision, instant retrieval |
| **Warm** (40-80%) | f16 | 2x | Slight compression, imperceptible latency |
| **Cool** (10-40%) | PQ8 | 8x | Smart quantization, ~1ms overhead |
| **Cold** (1-10%) | PQ4 | 16x | Heavy compression, still fast search |
| **Archive** (<1%) | Binary | 32x | Maximum density, batch retrieval |

**Memory Usage (1M Vectors, 384 Dimensions):**

| Configuration | Memory | Recall |
|---------------|--------|--------|
| Full Precision (f32) | ~1.5GB | 100% |
| Scalar Quantization | ~400MB | 98% |
| Product Quantization | ~200MB | 95% |
| Binary Quantization | ~50MB | 85% |
